speaker: interviewer
text: This is an interview with Paul Kornmehl. It is the 15th of February, 1996. Paul, you were telling me you had contact with your sister in Marseille.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I rang my sister, listened to her voice. She listened to my voice. I think-- [CRYING] [INAUDIBLE]. [CRYING] Anyway, so she couldn't travel, because you needed, in France, a sauf-conduit. A sauf-conduit stands for that you needed a, a passport-- not a passport, a piece of paper from the police-- if you traveled more than two or three kilometers, which was, for her, difficult to get. Later on, I found some ways of getting it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she sent over a French woman, a Jewish French woman. I don't remember her name, but she was very, very good. Sorry. Let me just concentrate. She was very, very good.

speaker: interviewee
text: This woman went with me to the head of the prefecture. And she offered some money for the [FRENCH]. The [FRENCH] stands for that he put it in his pocket. And Melitta was there. I was there. The Eislands were there, the Eislands, as far as I remember, and this woman. I don't remember her name. And there was a box. And she told me, that Frenchwoman-- I spoke French-- and how much money I should put in that box. And I put it in in such a way that it didn't fall in, but that he could take it out and put it in his pocket. And he gave us a sauf-conduit to travel to Lyon, for us and for the Eislands.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we traveled to Lyon, and there was the honorary Dutch consul. His name was Jacquet. I never knew his first name, Mr. Jacquet. And I was there, and he was the most wonderful man I've ever met in my life.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said to him, I haven't got a passport. He said, I'll fix that. He was the honorary consul, but his living he made by being the agent for the Netherlands for an insurance company called the [NON-ENGLISH], the Netherlands of 1845. We stayed in the Hotel Perrache. And within a very short time-- and my passport is here. I will tell you when we got the passport.

speaker: interviewee
text: I discussed with him everything. We had a problem. They gave us a Dutch passport. We were in the middle of the naturalization in Holland, so we were not entitled to have a Dutch passport. But he fixed that, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the passport was given out by the Swedish consul, because the Swedes were the representatives of the Dutch government in Vichy, France. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Within days I found some French people. I found some Jewish people in Lyon. And they told me that I should be very careful, because when you are in a hotel, you have to fill out a form that goes to the police. I didn't know, which I only know after the war, since a few years time that there was the Butcher of Lyon. And you probably heard about that, surely. If he was there in 1941, '42, I'm not sure about. I spoke to my sister in Marseille every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: What month would this have been?

speaker: interviewee
text: We're talking December, 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm.

speaker: interviewee
text: January.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, December, 1941. I got to know the police around the corner in the arrondissement. And they made it clear they will give me-- or I asked them, they didn't make it clear-- that I give them so many cigarettes-- because money was not so important. It was important, but only in American dollars. And the cigarette, which was apart from the Gauloise, was a cigarette called Balto. For three packets of cigarettes, I got a sauf-conduit for 100 kilometers. For six packets of cigarettes, I got unlimited sauf-conduit, 800 kilometers. And I needed to go from Lyon to Marseille. Why I want to go to Marseille, because I want to see my sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in December, we went to Marseille. By the way, again, I wrote a letter to Leizar. In arriving in Marseille, my sister in the hotel-- we stayed in the Hotel Select-- got a room for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was cooking on the sly, because there was nothing to eat in the restaurant. We were hungry. We were starving. In the restaurant, we needed tickets, as we were not residents.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have to go back to Lyon in a few weeks time. We didn't have any tickets. So even if we would have tickets, you can't buy anything with it. There was already-- how would I say? A famine or-- the, the people were already starving. Already by the end of '41, beginning of '42, they were starving.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in that hotel. We discussed everything. I was together with my sister. And I said to her, I have to get married the Jewish way, which I did. They organized it with Isaac's father, who was there in a little room, the old-fashioned way. It was a rabbi from somewhere. I had to give him so much money. The bride had to walk around me, and I had to walk around there. And we broke a glass. And that was we got married the Jewish way.

speaker: interviewer
text: What date was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in December, 1941. Don't ask me the date, because I really don't know. And I haven't got these papers anymore. How would I? Then I went back to Lyon, because I started to like Jacquet. And I met a man called Sally Noach who just arrived. I said Sally Noach was a very capable man. I think he was a Dutchman, but he came from Entwerp. But I might be wrong. He might also have been from Amsterdam. I introduced him to Jacquet. And Sally Noach was a terrific help for Jacquet. He helped hundreds and probably thousands of people. I myself met [? vis-a-vis ?] a man called Diamond. And this man Diamond had a contact who was the police. I'm sorry. I made a mistake. This has happened before I went to Marseille, I met Diamond, and-- because otherwise, we wouldn't have traveled. He had a contact to the police and he filled in a piece of paper that we made an application to the police to stay in Lyon. And the police gave the stamp on it. And that legalized you as being in Lyon. And if you get a sauf-conduit, wherever it was. It speaks by itself that I introduced this Diamond-- and he had a partner, but I can't think of his name. And the price which we paid could have been 10,000 francs, which wasn't really so much. He was a wonderful man. I don't know if he survived that war. And that piece of paper from the Diamond, from Mr. Diamond, went through the whole of our family who came out afterwards from Holland. The first one to arrive from Holland was my brother-in-law, Max Wolf, or Wolf, and my sister Bertha and the two sons. Again, the same story. They arrived at the demarcation line. And there was some money for the [NON-ENGLISH]. I don't know if they went via [INAUDIBLE] or anywhere else. But we found out if you give money for the [NON-ENGLISH], which was for the pocket, you get to Lyon. The first thing I did, Mr. Diamond got them the papers, the, the request to the police with a stamp. And this was not easy. Nobody else could have done it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went-- so after I was in Marseille for a few weeks, I went back to Lyon. Why I went back to Lyon? Yes, I think I was waiting for the rest of the family to arrive. The sauf-conduit which I had, I couldn't renew it with the man at the police. So what we did, we found some-- the color of that sauf-conduit was this color, right? How would you call this? Pink.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pink. And so we found some liquid where you can take the name out. And instead of Marseilles, you could put in the [FRENCH]. Then you can add to the [FRENCH] another name on sauf-conduit. All right, how would you translate sauf-conduit in English? A [GERMAN] in German, a piece of paper to travel, a travel document.

speaker: interviewer
text: A permit.

speaker: interviewee
text: Permit. Travel document. Anyway, we added this to it, not because we were stingy for the Balto, it also was important because we didn't make-- want to make up the money which we had with us. And we changed the money wherever we can. I went to Annecy with Melitta, and then I came back. Annecy was close to the Swiss border. And I went by myself to Annecy, without telling Melitta. I said, I'm leaving in the morning. I'm coming back in the evening. And I went to a place called Annemasse. In Annemasse, I went to a convent. And there was half of the convent was on the Swiss side. You can look it up in the-- in the-- Annemasse-- where Annemasse is now is half-Swiss and half-French. And I spoke to the Mother Superior. And I said, we have got a lot of people coming here. And they need to go to Switzerland. See, when you got to that convent and you were 10 kilometers inside Switzerland, they didn't-- the Swiss police didn't do anything to you. Once they caught you within 10 kilometers, then you were lost, because it is an international law. If you come from one country, you have to send them back to that country. So France libre, that so-called unoccupied France, when you came, they should deliver you back to [INAUDIBLE]. But they didn't do that. They delivered you to the Germans. The Swiss-- and I would like to make that statement-- were very, very pro-German during wartime, because they allowed the trains from Germany to go right through their territory-- if they were neutral, they shouldn't allow that-- to go to Italy. This is my opinion.

speaker: interviewee
text: Joseph Schmidt went over the border. He got caught. And he was a famous man. And they put him on the floor. He got TB. You know what TB is? And he died within four weeks. He was a famous singer, Joseph Schmidt. If I got that name wrong, then I will correct that later on. I went back to Lyon, all time changing the sauf-conduit. I got some new ones in between. But naturally, if you were holding it up with this color, you could see there is a little bit of-- how would you call it-- forgery in there, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I bought some new ones from another policeman. And I went to Jacquet and I told him about the monastery. We had a lot of officers coming from Holland, a lot. I would say a few hundred every month. I was eight or nine months in France. As long as I was in Lyon, there were, every month, 20, 30 which I know of. They came to our place, because we bought the tickets on the black market. We gave them something to eat, and they went to Annecy. They went to Annemasse. They got caught before Annemasse, because they didn't have the right sauf-conduit. They took a risk, and I couldn't get them more sauf-conduits. Then Jacquet went to get them out of prison, or Sally Noach went to get them out of prison, because Jacquet got him a diplomatic passport. How he did that, I don't know. I saved a lot of-- I saved, I would say Jacquet saved a lot of people. But I gave them the route, the way to Switzerland.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people altogether would [? Jacquet ?] [? and ?] yourself--

speaker: interviewee
text: There were hundreds of people. They went to Switzerland. I didn't want to go to Switzerland, because I was convinced that Switzerland is going to be occupied by the Germans. I was convinced about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in the month of December, my brother and sister-in-law arrived from-- and the two children. They stayed in Lyon for a short while. I got them the sauf-conduit. and they went, I think, either by train or by bus to Nice. They want to stay in Nice. But they got this application form, all right, stamped by the police.

speaker: interviewee
text: In March-- everything I did went via Leizar. In March, 1942, my brother and-- married in Amsterdam. And immediately after the marriage, my father and mother, within one or two weeks, arrived also in [NON-ENGLISH]. My sister Anna, who spoke excellent French, got also sauf-conduit. Between the two of them, between the two of us, we picked up our parents. We took them to Lyon. We got them that piece of paper.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we took them via bus, because we were not trusting that piece of paper, right? Via bus, via Annecy over the Route Napoleon to Nice, my parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we were waiting for my brother, who I think only arrived in June, July, with his new wife. Again, I picked them up myself, or maybe my sister with me. That-- I can't remember that. And they also went to Nice, not to Marseille.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, the next step was, how to get out of France? Now, I-- there was a place in Lyon called Place Bellecour. And I knew by that time some refugees. I didn't know local French people.

speaker: interviewee
text: I only know one couple who put up a lot of Jewish people, a hotel where you didn't have to fill in the form. And he didn't give us any advice. So I got one visa which was legal. And that visa cost 50, 100 francs. I went up and down to Marseille. In Marseille, in Hotel Select, and I'm going to the months of April, May, June, we had a toilet where we took the toilet out and put it in a machine, in Hotel Select, which is still there today. Naturally, it's refurbished. And in there we put in a printing machine. We had two printers. One, I got the name-- I think one name was Rosenblit, but I have to look it up, because I wrote it down this morning. I rang Melitta and find out the name. And we made visas for Mexico. We made visas for Panama. We made visas for all countries, not for money, for us and for friends. And at the end, we made a visa, the sortie, which means an exit permit, which I have got in my passport. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went also then-- before doing that, I went also to Nice. And I had some contacts to the police. And they told me that there will be a razia tonight. So I was in a hotel with 150 rooms. I knew about 20 or 30 Jewish people living there, and I put a piece of paper under the door.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was in the months of July, August. I have written it down. Should I go and get that paper? No.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll get it later on. 1942. You know what a razia is? They're going to pick up the people and do with them whatever they liked. So that was Vichy, France. [NON-ENGLISH]. I put that under the door and we left for Monaco. That means my father, my mother, Moses Wolf, Max Wolf, Bertha, the two boys, and Melitta and myself. We went to Monaco, because we thought Monaco is a free state and they can't touch you there. Two o'clock in the morning, knock on the door. The police. And I showed them that piece of paper from-- which I had from the police in Lyon. They said, oh, that's all right. Then they went upstairs. My father and mother was all right, same. But my brother-in-law was born in Poland. And sister and the two boys they took with them to the police.

speaker: interviewee
text: They took us because he cried out for me. He said to me, Paul, Paul, come and help me, right? Paul, Paul, come and help me. They took us also to the police.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we went to the police, I showed them the piece of paper from, from Lyon, and so they let us go. But I saw these trucks and buses. And I took Melitta back to the hotel. And I don't know if I took a taxi to follow them or what happened. I don't really know. But I had money in my pocket. They were all taken to the station in Nice. And I arrived in Nice speaking French reasonably well. At the time I spoke better French than I do now. I spoke to one of the guides there, policeman. And I gave him a certain amount of money. And I said, I want these four to come out. That transport, which was the first transport from France libre, unoccupied France, went to Poland. 40,000 people were transported from the south of France. Within two or three months, the, the [FRENCH], where the people were put in a train to Poland. Where they went, I don't know, but they never came back. My sister was the only one with my brother-in-law and the two boys that came out of it. I didn't see them anymore, but I knew they were free, so I went back to Monaco to pick up Melitta. This was a sign that we had to leave that France libre. And there was no talk that the Germans are going to occupy that place. So actually, I was wrong in saying the [NON-ENGLISH] was made later. It was our next station. I'd like to have a look when it was made, because I got it there. I went back to Marseille. We made a [NON-ENGLISH] for all our friends, for the whole family. There was no talk that we should go to, to Switzerland, because Switzerland's going to be occupied. We are talking 1942.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my sister Anna, her husband Isaac, Paulette the daughter, and my father and mother, and a cousin called [NON-ENGLISH] left for Perpignan with a sauf-conduit which came from-- which came from Lyon, because we didn't have the contacts in Marseille. I went up and down to get it.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Perpignan, they paid $1,000 for an ambulance to go to the Pyrenees. The, the passeur waited there for them. They made the mistake of paying him half immediately. He took them up in the mountains. [? Jakob ?] [NON-ENGLISH] carried my mother on his back. He also carried Paulette, right, on and off. And when they were up there, he took the other half and the passeur disappeared. The arrangement was that I'm going to wait until I hear when they arrive in Barcelona, or wherever in Spain. I never had that confirmation. They left in the month of September. I never-- Leizar, I didn't hear anything. So I got really worried. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My brother and sister-in-law left a week later. I didn't hear anything from them either, the same way I think. I didn't check that up with my sister-in-law, because this morning it was too early to ring them. It would have been the middle of the night. I left on the 6th or 8th of October. 6Th or 8th of October I left. We had a [NON-ENGLISH]. We had transit visas. It was all-- oh, yes. I forgot the most important thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: I got-- was called up to the perfecture Marseille. Yes, I forgot that. I'm sorry. And I was asked, you have got a [NON-ENGLISH]? Because we had to put that up that we are-- and I said yes, I've got a [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: And who else has got the [NON-ENGLISH], they knew. Melitta had a [NON-ENGLISH]. A fellow which I made it for, [? Notovic, ?] [? Klinge, ?] and my brother-in-law had visas. We all were put straight into prison. It was an old castle, which they took us down that castle, which is still today a prison, but I-- I can't remember the name of--

speaker: interviewer
text: What date was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was two weeks before we left for Spain, all of us. Three weeks before we left. That must have been in September, beginning of September. And they released Melitta immediately, within 12 hours, because I told them she's not well. They still had respect for women. [COUGHS] The other ones were released, including my brother-in-law, because he was worried about his hat, because he bought himself a new hat to travel to Spain and he was an Englishman. So he was-- and he put that on paper. We still laughed about that. They were released. But the man from Vichy came and he was from [NON-ENGLISH]. And he interviewed me. He spoke-- [NON-ENGLISH] was a province, which was before the First World War, German, and after the First World War, it was France. And after with the-- during the Hitler time, even before they invaded Holland, Belgium, and France, they took [NON-ENGLISH] back. He spoke German and French, this man. He interviewed me. And in the beginning, he was very pleasant to me. He said, you made these [NON-ENGLISH]. I said, no. I went to a coffee shop. And somebody said he can get me. He knows somebody in the prefecture, he can get it. But the only place where you can get a [NON-ENGLISH] was the prefecture. He said, no. You made it. And then I can't tell you-- the interrogation if this lasted 10 hours, 12 hours. For me, it was a nightmare. At the end, I was so tired. And they had some lamps, like this lamp there. They had a few shining on me. And I didn't know what they were doing, but he took a chair, which was a timber chair, and he crashed it over my head. Surely, I didn't feel anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't, didn't feel anything, because you get to the stage where you are-- nothing can really happen to you. All I knew I had this [NON-ENGLISH] from a man in a coffee shop. And I knew even the coffee shop, the name of the coffee shop, right? We're going to change the tape now, Paul.  We'll come back to this story.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to change the tape.
